glossary.definition: the ability to learn, adapt and make decisions based on information from the environment
glossary.sentence: In this topic, you will find out what we understand by intelligence, how artificial intelligence is defined, and what is a fundamental approach to assessment of intelligence of a computer system.

glossary.definition: most commonly used as a standard for the development of intelligent computer systems
glossary.sentence: Firstly, in one of the first topics of the study course, we have already found that human intelligence is most commonly used as a standard for the development of intelligent computer systems.

glossary.definition: the ability of animals to learn, adapt, and make decisions based on information from their environment
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence . It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows.

glossary.definition: the ability of plants to learn, adapt, and make decisions based on information from their environment
glossary.sentence: Knowledge about plant intelligence is even more limited. It is currently known that plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system (Parise, Gagliano and Souza, 2020) .

glossary.definition: Developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience (Copeland, 2020).

glossary.definition: an approach to developing intelligent computer systems that focuses on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system.

glossary.definition: A test intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence by evaluating its ability to exhibit intelligent behavior equivalent to, or indistinguishable from, that of a human.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: an ideal standard of intelligent performance, based on logical rules and reasoning
glossary.sentence: the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4).

glossary.definition: Entities capable to perceive its environment and to act within it, acting to achieve the best outcome or the best expected outcome when there is uncertainty.
glossary.sentence: In this context, an agent is any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it. A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: The ability of a computer system to process, understand, and generate natural language, enabling successful communication with humans.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does;

glossary.definition: the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems
glossary.sentence: In general, "knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems" (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: The ability of a computer system to use stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : ... automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions;

glossary.definition: A method of data analysis that automates analytical model building, based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.”

glossary.definition: An extended version of the Turing test that requires a computer system to have computer vision to perceive objects and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations of problems and their solutions and rules for transforming these symbols
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations of problems and their solutions and rules for transforming these symbols.

glossary.definition: models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.

glossary.definition: The goal of understanding the principles that make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems, achieved through analysis, hypothesis formulation, testing, and designing computational systems.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions (Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : scientific goal is related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems. To achieve this goal, it is necessary to analyse manifestations of intelligent performance in different types of systems, to formulate and test hypotheses about what it takes to construct intelligent artefacts, as well as designing, building and experimenting with computational systems that are able of demonstrating intelligent performance;

glossary.definition: The goal of developing useful intelligent artefacts through the use of different paradigms, approaches, methods, and tasks.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions (Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : ... engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts. To achieve these goals, the field of artificial intelligence uses different paradigms, approaches, methods and tasks.

glossary.definition: A mechanism that enables intelligent behavior through feedback, which can be simulated by a computer.
glossary.sentence: The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer.

glossary.definition: Artificial systems that demonstrate intelligent performance and can operate under their own control.
glossary.sentence: The main research question is how intelligent artefacts can operate under their own control. ... designing, building and experimenting with computational systems that are able of demonstrating intelligent performance;

glossary.definition: The ability of a computer system to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: The ability of a computer system to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: has an unlimited capacity and provides a long-term storage of knowledge
glossary.sentence: Humans gain knowledge in different ways and keep it in the long-term memory. This memory has an unlimited capacity and provides a long-term storage of knowledge.

glossary.definition: A type of memory that stores knowledge processed at a specific time.
glossary.sentence: Humans use for this purpose long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge and short-term memory - to store knowledge processed at a specific time;

glossary.definition: defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence is based on the physical symbol system hypothesis , defined by Newell and Simon in 1976.

glossary.definition: simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units
glossary.sentence: This approach has emerged from attempts to mathematically model neural activity in the human brain and is based on so-called artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism.

glossary.definition: also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.

glossary.definition: an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated, related to the development of computer systems capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) .

glossary.definition: an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities, much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales, true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008).

glossary.definition: a paradigm under new artificial intelligence, considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning,

glossary.definition: based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: a graph that represents all possible states of a game
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: A mathematical or formal specification that uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: the process by which a person determines its initial state, goal state, and alternative courses of available actions
glossary.sentence: However, the problem-solving process is possible not only because a person is able to determine its initial state, goal state, and alternative courses of available actions, but also because humans use general knowledge of a problem domain when dealing with problems,

glossary.definition: The starting state of the problem-solving process or game.
glossary.sentence: In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid, which appears when you start the game (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: the desired final state of the search process
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge provides rules for choosing those paths in a state space graph that are most likely to lead to an acceptable problem solution

glossary.definition: The means available to solve a problem, such as time, money, or transportation.
glossary.sentence: Once we have defined the problem, we can start thinking about resources and opportunities available to us, which together allow the identification of actions that we can take to solve the problem.

glossary.definition: The possibilities or chances to solve a problem, such as using a bus, taxi, bicycle, or scooter.
glossary.sentence: Returning to our example of the job interview, you are more likely to evaluate the means of transport available to you, for example, you can use a bus, taxi, bicycle, or scooter.

glossary.definition: Changes the physical coordinates or location of the robot.
glossary.sentence: Each action of the robot changes its physical coordinates or, in other words, location.

glossary.definition: A set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: elements of the state space graph
glossary.sentence: Heuristic measures are assigned to nodes of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Represents the initial state or states of affairs in the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The robot starts at location C1, which we can represent as a root node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The goal state of a problem-solving process, which does not have descendants.
glossary.sentence: A state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: Intermediate states of a problem-solving process, which have both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: A state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: connections between nodes in the state space graph

glossary.definition: A way of structural organization of data about the real world.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world;

glossary.definition: Places where to store the data, such as files, databases, and others.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: ... data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others;

glossary.definition: Ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: ... algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: nodes in the state space graph that represent the end of the game

glossary.definition: Represents a specific location or state that the robot can reach from another location.

glossary.definition: An application of a state space graph used to represent games.
glossary.sentence: Topic 2.2 - A Game Tree - an application of a state space graph

glossary.term: Zero-Sum Deterministic Two-Person Games with Perfect Information
glossary.definition: A type of game where two players compete, have complete information about the game, and one player's loss equals another player's gain.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information .

glossary.definition: Games where randomness factors are not involved.
glossary.sentence: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information, such as checkers, tic-tac-toe and chess, in which both players have all the information about the course of the game (for example, which objects or figures are still available, which moves have been performed and which next moves are possible), and random factors are not involved.

glossary.definition: Games where randomness factors are involved, such as the use of a die.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information, such as backgammon, in which both players still are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.

glossary.definition: A type of game where both players have complete information about the course of the game.
glossary.sentence: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information, such as checkers, tic-tac-toe and chess, in which both players have all the information about the course of the game (for example, which objects or figures are still available, which moves have been performed and which next moves are possible), and random factors are not involved.

glossary.definition: A type of game where some information is hidden from one or both players.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: The current state of the game, represented by the board or grid.
glossary.sentence: Each mark added to the game grid changes the appearance of the grid.

glossary.definition: a point in the game tree that represents a state of the game
glossary.sentence: As a result, the algorithm goes up one level, and finds that the current node has another unconsidered descendant.

glossary.term: Deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where moves are made alternately and each player has complete knowledge of the game state.
glossary.sentence: Since moves are made alternately in a deterministic two-player game of perfect information, node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A set of nodes that represent states from which only one player can make a move.
glossary.sentence: One or more nodes are located at each level, and these display states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.

glossary.definition: Characteristics that define the state of a game, such as score, game board, etc.
glossary.sentence: These parameters can be described as follows: they are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game;

glossary.definition: performs three main steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node
glossary.sentence: In the previous topic of the course, you have already considered that any search algorithm performs three main steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node.

glossary.definition: The initial node where the search begins, denoted as A in the given text.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A.

glossary.definition: the target node that the algorithm is searching for
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if the search goal node exists among the nodes located in the OPEN list.

glossary.definition: the output of uninformed search algorithms
glossary.sentence: The algorithms provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: Nodes that have been generated by the search algorithm but whose descendants or ancestors have not yet been generated.
glossary.sentence: Two open nodes - C2 and B1 - appear.

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants or ancestors have already been generated by the search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, the node C1 becomes a closed node.

glossary.definition: a type of search that begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found.
glossary.sentence: in data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory;

glossary.definition: a type of search that starts with a goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it; typically used in tasks of diagnosis when it is known what happened and it is necessary to understand reasons for it.
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory;

glossary.definition: a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred; typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation.
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it; corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Initial nodes in the state space graph that represent the starting points of the search process.
glossary.sentence: If it is data-driven search, then the search start node is selected from the set of root nodes, but the search goal node– from the set of leaf nodes.

glossary.definition: a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, used by other search algorithms to find a solution path more effectively
glossary.sentence: Backtracking gives answers to the questions about the repeatedly discovered nodes and cycles.

glossary.definition: rarely include other steps and receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data
glossary.sentence: Uninformed search algorithms rarely include other steps. Therefore, uninformed search algorithms receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: algorithms that differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform, receiving an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or a heuristic evaluation function, and having two additional steps - evaluating generated ancestors/descendants by applying a heuristic evaluation function and selecting the best node and/or ordering the nodes according to heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform (Figure 2). Heuristic search algorithms receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function. Moreover, heuristic search algorithms have two additional steps - after expanding the current node, they evaluate the generated ancestors/descendants (depending on the search direction) by applying a heuristic evaluation function and then select the best node and/or order the nodes according to heuristic measures.

glossary.definition: a search strategy used in alpha-beta pruning to explore the state space graph
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011), in which it starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: a search strategy that explores all nodes at a given depth before moving to the next depth level
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: a search strategy that uses two simultaneous searches: one starting from the initial state and one from the goal state
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: an example of a heuristic search algorithm that explores a state space graph by iteratively moving to the next node with the highest quality until the goal node is reached
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing, Best-First Search, and Beam Search will be considered as examples of algorithms in this category in our course.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that may be slower than the heuristic evaluation function-based algorithm
glossary.sentence: Although this algorithm may lead to the search goal node faster than Best-First Search, there are not any guidelines on how the beam width should be chosen.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that balances exploration and efficiency by selecting a limited number of promising nodes (based on their heuristic measures) for expansion in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: a node that has no descendants
glossary.sentence: It reaches a leaf node in which the minimiser has the highest score and therefore wins the game.

glossary.definition: a sequence of nodes in a state space graph that forms a loop, where a node can be revisited multiple times
glossary.sentence: In this case, the search algorithm can get stuck in the cycle and never find the search goal node.

glossary.definition: a data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order
glossary.sentence: A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: a list that stores nodes to be expanded, along with their heuristic measures
glossary.sentence: They are added to the OPEN list together with their heuristic measures in an ordered way.

glossary.definition: a list that stores already expanded or generated nodes
glossary.sentence: The nodes E and H are moved to the CLOSED list as already expanded nodes.

glossary.definition: the node that the algorithm is currently evaluating
glossary.sentence: It finds that this node does not have other unconsidered descendants, and therefore acquires the precise heuristic measure of its descendant, i.e. a value of −1.

glossary.definition: a type of data structure that works according to the Last in, First Out principle
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A step in the search process where nodes are expanded, evaluated, and added to or removed from the OPEN and CLOSED lists.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 0: The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: a problem in Depth-First Search where the algorithm searches for the search goal node for a long time in depth before returning to higher levels
glossary.sentence: Besides that, the algorithm has another serious shortcoming—the problem of so-called indefinitely deep paths . It means that in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.

glossary.definition: a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths, with a depth limit that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search is a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: the length of the longest path in a state space graph
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: indicates that there is a depth limit that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored
glossary.sentence: “limited” indicates that there is a depth limit that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored.

glossary.definition: defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored, should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation
glossary.sentence: The depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation.

glossary.definition: nodes located at the depth limit
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: to consider nodes sequentially at the same level before going deeper
glossary.sentence: “breadth” means to consider nodes sequentially at the same level before going deeper;

glossary.definition: indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation
glossary.sentence: “first” indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation.

glossary.definition: labels assigned to each node indicating its depth level, used to determine if a node should be considered a leaf/root node
glossary.sentence: Depth labels allow the algorithm "to understand" if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: the depth of a node, calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of its parent node

glossary.definition: a type of data structure that follows the First-In-First-Out principle
glossary.sentence: A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 18).

glossary.definition: Nodes generated by expanding a given node, potentially added to the OPEN list for further exploration.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: a set of rules used to evaluate the performance and make predictions
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: a function used to estimate the value of a node in a game tree based on a set of predefined rules

glossary.definition: knowledge used in the development of intelligent computer systems for problems that may have an exact solution but lack sufficient computational resources
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is often used in the development of intelligent computer systems for problems that may have an exact solution, but there are not enough computational resources to find that solution.

glossary.definition: a search strategy that uses heuristics to guide the search process
glossary.sentence: The effectiveness of finding the search goal node can be improved by using heuristic search algorithms.

glossary.definition: the set of all possible states or situations that can be explored during the search process
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge provides rules for choosing those paths in a state space graph that are most likely to lead to an acceptable problem solution

glossary.definition: a search strategy that does not use any additional information about the search space

glossary.definition: values assigned to nodes in the state space graph to evaluate the game state

glossary.definition: a function that represents the heuristic evaluation of a node n
glossary.sentence: This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration;

glossary.definition: the distance between the search start node and node n

glossary.definition: an algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph, with the idea of climbing up to the best heuristic measure, which in the best case would be defined for the search goal node, and stops when the search goal node is reached or a node whose heuristic measure is better than its descendants/ancestors is found.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph. If we imagine that the state space graph with heuristic measures of nodes is similar to a mountain, then the search goal node can be considered to be the top of the mountain, while the search start node is the foot of the mountain (Figure 4). In this case, the Hill Climbing algorithm will always climb up to the best heuristic measure, which in the best case would be defined for the search goal node.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that selects the best node found so far and keeps all previously found nodes, allowing it to switch between different paths in the state space graph and avoiding local maximum and plateau problems.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it. In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: a problem that occurs when the Hill Climbing algorithm finds a node with a better heuristic measure than its descendants/ancestors and stops, without reaching the search goal node.

glossary.definition: a problem that occurs when the Hill Climbing algorithm cannot continue the search because the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
