glossary.definition: values that are numerical and can be measured, such as the average mark in the semester work or the number of missed lectures
glossary.sentence: The features “Average mark in the semester work” and “The number of missed lectures” have continuous values, therefore the Euclidean distance is calculated.

glossary.definition: values that are non-numerical and represent categories or labels, such as laboratory work evaluations
glossary.sentence: In turn, the features that represent the student's evaluations in the laboratory works have categorical values, so the Hamming distance is used to determine whether the feature values of the data objects are the same or different.

glossary.definition: a version of the kNN algorithm where only one nearest neighbor is considered for classification
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: an algorithm that uses the concept of similarity to group data objects into clusters
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity was considered in detail in the second topic of the module in regard to the kNN algorithm.

glossary.definition: a method used by the kNN algorithm to classify new data objects based on the majority vote of their k nearest neighbors
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: a process of scaling feature values to a common range, usually between 0 and 1, to prevent features with large ranges from dominating the model
glossary.sentence: kNN algorithm typically demands the normalization of feature value. Two common methods for normalisation are the following:

glossary.definition: a group of data objects that are similar to each other
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity was considered in detail in the second topic of the module in regard to the kNN algorithm.

glossary.definition: a measure used to determine how close two data objects are to each other
glossary.sentence: It is also used in unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning where the algorithm learns patterns and relationships in the data without prior knowledge of the expected output
glossary.sentence: It is also used in unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: the characteristics or attributes of the data objects being clustered
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity depends on the feature types: for categorical feature values, usually, the Hamming distance is used that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ: the fewer the differences in features, the greater the similarity of data objects;

glossary.definition: measures used to calculate the geometric distance between continuous feature values
glossary.sentence: for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that groups data objects into clusters based on their similarity
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: the center of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid.

glossary.definition: an approach to building a hierarchy of clusters by merging smaller clusters into larger ones
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters.

glossary.definition: an approach to building a hierarchy of clusters by splitting larger clusters into smaller ones
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach.

glossary.definition: methods used to measure the similarity between clusters and decide the rules for merging or splitting them
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods.

glossary.definition: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.
glossary.sentence: The most used methods are (Hastie, 2017) : Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height;
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height;

glossary.definition: terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: serves as an indicator of cluster similarity: Two data objects (leaves) in the same clade are more similar than two data objects (leaves) in another clade.
glossary.sentence: The height of clades serves as an indicator of cluster similarity: Two data objects (leaves) in the same clade are more similar than two data objects (leaves) in another clade.

glossary.definition: usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: can be performed at different levels of the hierarchy leading to a different number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: Cut-offs can be performed at different levels of the hierarchy leading to a different number of clusters.

glossary.definition: measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values.

glossary.definition: applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable,
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”.

glossary.definition: is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.

glossary.definition: in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones
glossary.sentence: The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.

glossary.definition: a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1)
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0)
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1)
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1);

glossary.definition: a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0)
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0),

glossary.definition: a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: an averaging technique used to evaluate performance in multiclass classification
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging.

glossary.definition: an averaging technique used to evaluate performance in multiclass classification
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging.

glossary.definition: evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.

glossary.term: Recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate)
glossary.definition: evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: The ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false positives. A high precision means that most of the predicted positive instances are actually positive.
glossary.sentence: you would probably prefer a classifier that rejects many good videos (low recall) but keeps only safe ones (high precision)

glossary.definition: The ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives. A high recall means that most of the actual positive instances are predicted as positive.
glossary.sentence: you would probably prefer a classifier that rejects many good videos (low recall) but keeps only safe ones (high precision)

glossary.definition: The tradeoff between precision and recall, where increasing one often decreases the other.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff.

glossary.definition: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, used to evaluate the performance of a classifier.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A plot of false positive rate vs true positive rate for different thresholds, used to evaluate the performance of a classifier.
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A measure of the performance of a classifier, calculated as the area under the ROC curve.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A measure of how similar an object is to its own cluster compared to other clusters.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score. The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: A plot of the Silhouette Score for each object in a cluster, used to visualize the separation between clusters.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot, representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: related to modifying weights in some particular way
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way .

glossary.definition: A cell in the human brain that receives and transmits signals through its dendrites and axon.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig.

glossary.definition: A branching extension of a neuron that receives signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: A long, thin extension of a neuron that transmits signals to other neurons or to muscles or glands.
glossary.sentence: The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.

glossary.definition: The gap between two neurons where chemical signals are transmitted from one neuron to another.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: a synapse having a positive weight
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight).

glossary.definition: a synapse having a negative weight
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight).

glossary.definition: the weight of a synapse that is used to calculate its strength
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight).

glossary.definition: the result of multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight).

glossary.definition: the minimum total input required for a neuron to fire
glossary.sentence: If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Berm\u00f9dez, 2014)).

glossary.definition: primarily consists of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the human mental activity consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: a system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location
glossary.sentence: According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), the human brain is a highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location.

glossary.definition: associated with synapses
glossary.sentence: Learning in human neural networks is associated with synapses.

glossary.definition: increases when the synapse is used often and decreases when it is not active
glossary.sentence: It is known that a synapse is made stronger by the however much more electrical current it must carry. Stronger here means that the synapse has a higher conductivity.

glossary.definition: a mathematical model of a neuron defined by McCulloch and Pitts in 1943
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons
glossary.sentence: In general, an artificial neuron has (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons.

glossary.definition: real-valued weights that express the strength or importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output
glossary.sentence: a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output.

glossary.definition: a numerical value that can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons
glossary.sentence: an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.

glossary.definition: a function for combining input signals
glossary.sentence: for example, the summation function );

glossary.definition: a function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships
glossary.sentence: a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships;

glossary.definition: added to each layer of the network.
glossary.sentence: At every layer, we also add a bias input.

glossary.definition: individual artificial neurons in an artificial neural network
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: layers of neurons between input and output layers that contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers . The input layer does not contain computing neurons because it only receives inputs signals from the environment and passes them to the hidden layers.

glossary.definition: a network with connections only in one direction, where every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir \u2022 ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: a network that feeds its outputs back into its inputs, forming a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or chaotic behaviour
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: applied by each processing unit after calculating the weighted sum, including the bias input.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: to modify the weights of the network
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: simple processing units
glossary.sentence: a set of simple processing units (artificial neurons );

glossary.definition: the pattern of connectivity of processing units
glossary.sentence: a pattern of connectivity of processing units;

glossary.definition: resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little
glossary.sentence: According to ( Borisovs et al., 1998), at the same time, artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little.

glossary.definition: it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: identifying the structure or pattern in data
glossary.sentence: pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.definition: projecting probable consequences of given situations
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): ... Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: finding the "best" organization of constraints
glossary.sentence: optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints;

glossary.definition: separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal
glossary.sentence: noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables
glossary.sentence: non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: has only one processing layer; all the inputs are connected directly to the outputs
glossary.sentence: The title of the network – “a single layer perceptron” – emphasizes that the network has only one processing layer; all the inputs are connected directly to the outputs.

glossary.definition: used for computing the weight correction
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule :

glossary.definition: one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: a neural network that has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: It has (Negnevitsky, 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers.

glossary.definition: sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons;
glossary.sentence: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons;

glossary.definition: consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: computing units in the neural network, connected to all neurons of the next layer but not to neurons at the same layer.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron is connected to all neurons of the next layer. However, neurons at the same layer are not connected.

glossary.definition: used for learning in multilayer perceptron, based on two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig.).
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky, 2011): assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: the propagation of inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network;

glossary.definition: a task where the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: a system that uses knowledge to solve problems
glossary.sentence: It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: only knowledge relevant and important for tasks that the system will solve should be acquired and described
glossary.sentence: The last definition mentions two important aspects. Firstly, "full range" does not mean that the developer of the intelligent system should describe all knowledge available in our world.

glossary.definition: knowledge should be described in a formal (mathematical) way with the aim of allowing the intelligent system to use and process it
glossary.sentence: Secondly, knowledge should be described in a formal (mathematical) way with the aim of allowing the intelligent system to use and process it.

glossary.definition: the whole set of rules and knowledge that the system uses to make decisions
glossary.sentence: in each iteration, the inference mechanism searches the whole knowledge base.

glossary.definition: the mechanism that processes the knowledge stored in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: stores unrelated facts
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: used to describe knowledge
glossary.sentence: The inference engine depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: governing the behaviour of a complex environment
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept
glossary.sentence: structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept;

glossary.term: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: theories that postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: an essential feature of frame-based expert systems, ensures that sub-class and instance frames can inherit slots and their values from class frames
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames.

glossary.definition: represented by a title of “is a” and allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”).

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “is an instance/example of” and allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “Part of” and allows relating parts of the object to the whole object
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of”.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “property/has a property of” and allows relating objects/concepts to their properties
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “value” and allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”.

glossary.definition: based on principles: acquire knowledge, explore relations, represent knowledge in structured way, support inheritance
glossary.sentence: Semantic networks have a number of advantages: they allow ease of representation and understanding of knowledge; they allow defining a variety of relations between objects/ concepts; they represent knowledge in a structured way; they support inheritance; they have a simple inference engine.

glossary.definition: knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts
glossary.sentence: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts );

glossary.definition: well-defined semantics to avoid ambiguity during interpretation of semantic network
glossary.sentence: they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways;

glossary.definition: uncertainty or multiple possible interpretations during knowledge representation or interpretation of semantic networks
glossary.sentence: they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways;

glossary.definition: specific type of knowledge that cannot be represented by semantic networks
glossary.sentence: they do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge like negation, disjunction, or also all/part/several/nobody/many, etc.

glossary.definition: specific type of knowledge that cannot be represented by semantic networks
glossary.sentence: they do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge like negation, disjunction, or also all/part/several/nobody/many, etc.

glossary.definition: data-structure for representing stereotyped situations or typical knowledge about objects/concepts
glossary.sentence: A frame is “a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation” (Minsky, 1975).

glossary.definition: properties of objects/concepts in frames
glossary.sentence: The frame has a specific structure (Fig.2) that includes: slot titles, slot values and facets ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames
glossary.sentence: Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: extended knowledge about slot values and instructions for inference engine on how to process properties of objects/concepts
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: system that uses frames to capture and represent knowledge
glossary.sentence: Frames are used to capture and represent knowledge in a frame-based expert system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: frame containing common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts
glossary.sentence: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: frames derived from other classes, containing new slots characterizing objects belonging to sub-class
glossary.sentence: The sub-class frames can contain new slots that are not included in the class frames but characterize objects belonging to the sub-class.

glossary.definition: allow creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames
glossary.sentence: As a result, the class frames allow creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: relate two frames by using a unique title of one frame as a slot value in another frame
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame
glossary.sentence: Three types of relations are used to relate frames ( Negnevitsky , 2011): Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame.

glossary.definition: represents extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: defines any other type of relations by using the referencing principle
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: a slot can have a procedural attachment, such as regular procedures and/or demons
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.

glossary.definition: a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: acquired when demons are assigned to frames
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules
glossary.sentence: The most popular expert systems are rule-based systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011). A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: Used to develop rule-based systems. They represent a set of problem-solving instructions not a knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems. They represent a set of problem-solving instructions not a knowledge about objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: a set of rules used by the expert system to make decisions, which can be difficult to manage when there are many rules
glossary.sentence: in the case of the considerable number of production rules, it is difficult to find out the contribution of each specific rule to the problem-solving process;

glossary.definition: Rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: a component of the expert system where data is temporarily stored during the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory;

glossary.definition: Implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.
glossary.sentence: It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Makes the basis of the inference mechanism. Implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle 'Recognize-act' makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: Used to make the search more effective. Three types: control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: the process by which the expert system selects and fires production rules; consists of two stages: "Recognize" and "act".
glossary.sentence: the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base;

glossary.definition: the set of enabled rules that are able to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a single "chunk" of problem-solving knowledge, the rule content can provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action.
glossary.sentence: In addition, because each rule corresponds to a single “chunk” of problem-solving knowledge, the rule content can provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action.

glossary.definition: adding confidence factors to represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: knowledge that is not complete or certain, which can be represented and reasoned with using confidence factors
glossary.sentence: Dealing with incomplete and uncertain knowledge. Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: a process that searches the whole knowledge base in each iteration, which can be computational resource-consuming
glossary.sentence: in each iteration, the inference mechanism searches the whole knowledge base. It could be a very computational resource-consuming process.

glossary.definition: the knowledge used by the system to solve problems, which can be represented in chunks
glossary.sentence: In addition, because each rule corresponds to a single “chunk” of problem-solving knowledge, the rule content can provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action.

glossary.definition: applications that require fast processing and response times, which may not be suitable for expert systems with many rules
glossary.sentence: Expert systems with a large set of rules (over 100 rules) can be slow, and thus can be unsuitable for real-time applications;

glossary.definition: expert systems that use a set of rules to make decisions, which have advantages and disadvantages
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors. However, rule-based expert systems have also disadvantages ( Negnevitsky , 2011):
